APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses a core NLP/semantics problem (metaphor identification) with a distributional/geometric modelling approach and direct comparisons to prior supervised and semi-supervised baselines, which fits a top NLP/ML venue. The methodology and evaluation (PMI vectors from Wikipedia, per-pair subspace construction, logistic regression, F-score against Gutiérrez et al. baselines) are appropriate for the community, but the absence of reported hyperparameters and no mention of released code/documentation weakens reproducibility. Given solid baselines and thorough related work positioning but limited reproducibility details, I judge it a good fit with minor concerns. | B(4): This paper addresses a core NLP problem (metaphor identification) with a novel geometric, context-sensitive modeling idea that enables zero-shot identification and compares against appropriate baselines on a sizable adjective–noun dataset, which makes it well aligned with top-tier NLP/ML audiences. The methodology (distributional vectors from Wikipedia, subspace selection, logistic regression) and evaluation (F-score against supervised and semi-supervised baselines) meet community expectations, though practical details (hyperparameters, code availability) and broader empirical validation beyond a single dataset are limited — hence a strong but not perfect fit.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a core NLP/semantics problem (metaphor identification) with a distributional/geometric modelling approach and direct comparisons to prior supervised and semi-supervised baselines, which fits a top NLP/ML venue. Given solid baselines and thorough related work positioning but limited reproducibility details, I judge it a good fit with minor concerns.

The methodology (distributional vectors from Wikipedia, subspace selection, logistic regression) and evaluation (F-score against supervised and semi-supervised baselines) meet community expectations, though practical details (hyperparameters, code availability) and broader empirical validation beyond a single dataset are limited — hence a strong but not perfect fit.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (geometric contextual model, zero-shot capability, and comparison to Gutiérrez et al. 2016). Notation and method descriptions appear clear enough to follow the workflow from PMI vector construction to subspace selection and logistic-regression evaluation, and related work coverage and writing quality are noted as good. Minor presentation issues remain: the summary does not indicate whether notation is fully formalized across all selection techniques, nor whether figures/tables and hyperparameter details are provided to aid reproducibility. From a presentation standpoint this reads as a solid paper with only small gaps that could be tightened for clarity. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (a geometric, context-sensitive subspace model for zero-shot adjective-noun metaphor identification) are stated early and carried through method and experiments. Notation appears consistent and related work coverage is thorough; figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and the writing quality is good. Presentation could be improved by reporting hyperparameters, code availability, and any limitations (none are stated), which would aid reproducibility and clarity for applied readers like myself. These omissions are relatively minor, so the paper is clear but not exceptional.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents a clear experimental setup and provides empirical comparisons showing F-scores comparable to a fully supervised baseline and superior to a semi-supervised variant, which supports the central claim that contextually generated subspaces can identify unseen metaphors. However, important methodological details needed for reproducibility and for assessing robustness are missing: hyperparameters and random seeds are not reported, code is not mentioned as available, and no statistical significance testing or ablation isolating each model component is reported. The mathematical approach appears plausible given the summary and multiple subspace selection techniques were explored, but the lack of detailed controls and statistical analysis prevents full confidence in the strength of the claims. | B(3): The paper presents a plausible, well-described geometric approach using PMI vectors from Wikipedia and per-pair k-dimensional subspace selection feeding a logistic regression classifier, and reports F-scores comparable to the supervised baseline of Gutiérrez et al. (2016). However, the summary indicates no mathematical derivations or proofs to verify correctness of the subspace selection rationale, hyperparameters are not reported, and there is no mention of statistical significance tests or code release, which weakens confidence in the experimental validity and reproducibility. These omissions raise moderate technical concerns but do not fundamentally invalidate the core empirical claim that the method can match prior supervised performance.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a clear experimental setup and provides empirical comparisons showing F-scores comparable to a fully supervised baseline and superior to a semi-supervised variant, which supports the central claim that contextually generated subspaces can identify unseen metaphors. However, important methodological details needed for reproducibility and for assessing robustness are missing: hyperparameters and random seeds are not reported, code is not mentioned as available, and no statistical significance testing or ablation isolating each model component is reported. The mathematical approach appears plausible given the summary and multiple subspace selection techniques were explored, but the lack of detailed controls and statistical analysis prevents full confidence in the strength of the claims.

However, the summary indicates no mathematical derivations or proofs to verify correctness of the subspace selection rationale, hyperparameters are not reported, and there is no mention of statistical significance tests or code release, which weakens confidence in the experimental validity and reproducibility. ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper advances a notable and distinguishable idea: generating a dedicated geometric subspace for each adjective–noun candidate and using only individual word vectors to enable zero-shot metaphor detection. This departs from prior supervised tensor-based methods (Gutiérrez et al. 2016) and from earlier extraction work (e.g., 2013) by emphasizing per-instance contextual subspaces rather than learned phrase representations, which is a non-trivial and conceptually clear contribution. While the approach builds on established distributional semantics, its framing (dynamic, ad-hoc subspace generation) and zero-shot claim would likely inform experts and represent a meaningful novelty rather than a merely incremental tweak. | B(4): The paper proposes a distinct, interpretable geometric formulation that generates a dedicated subspace for each adjective–noun pair and uses only individual word vectors to enable true zero-shot metaphor identification—this is a notable departure from supervised tensor-based constructions (Gutiérrez et al. 2016). The framing of metaphors as contextually induced subspaces and the demonstration that simple logistic regression on those subspaces matches supervised performance is a clear, novel perspective beyond routine application of distributional similarity. That said, prior work on extracting novel metaphors (e.g. 2013 efforts) addresses related goals, so the contribution is a strong and well-differentiated advance rather than a paradigm shift.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper provides a focused empirical evaluation (comparing to Gutiérrez et al.’s supervised and semi‑supervised baselines and simple baselines) and examines multiple k‑dimensional subspace selection techniques, which offers some ablation of model choices. However, experiments are limited to a single adjective–noun dataset and PMI vectors from one Wikipedia snapshot, with no hyperparameter details, random seeds, code, or statistical significance reported, so reproducibility and robustness are unclear. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, but the paper omits discussion of failure cases or limitations and lacks broader sensitivity analyses (e.g., different embeddings, dataset splits, or domain transfer such as in prior work on novel metaphor extraction). These gaps justify a middle rating. | B(3): The experiments show direct comparisons to the supervised and semi‑supervised baselines of Gutiérrez et al. (2016) and report comparable or superior F‑scores using only PMI vectors from Wikipedia, which supports the core zero‑shot claim. However, the paper omits important reproducibility details (no hyperparameters or code mentioned), does not discuss failure cases or limitations, and provides no clear ablation/sensitivity analysis beyond trying five subspace selection techniques. Related work is reported as thorough, but the empirical evaluation is missing analyses that would increase confidence in generalization to realistic novel data and in choice of design decisions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a focused empirical evaluation (comparing to Gutiérrez et al.’s supervised and semi‑supervised baselines and simple baselines) and examines multiple k‑dimensional subspace selection techniques, which offers some ablation of model choices. IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important problem—identifying unseen metaphors—by proposing a simple, general geometric contextualization that enables zero-shot classification using only individual word vectors (PMI vectors from Wikipedia). Demonstrating F-scores comparable to a fully supervised tensor model (Gutiérrez et al. 2016) and better than their semi‑supervised variant suggests this approach could change how researchers model contextual meaning for metaphors and encourage more work on per‑pair subspace methods. Its simplicity and reliance on widely available distributional vectors make it likely to be adopted and extended, though the lack of reported hyperparameters, code availability, and unstated limitations may slow immediate reproducibility and broader uptake. | B(4): The paper tackles a consequential problem—identifying metaphors, including unseen adjective–noun pairs—and proposes a novel geometric, context-sensitive subspace method that enables zero-shot classification using only word vectors. Demonstrating F-scores comparable to a fully supervised tensor baseline (Gutiérrez et al. 2016) suggests this approach could meaningfully shift practice toward lightweight, generalizable models for metaphor detection. The method opens concrete new directions (pair-specific subspace induction, PMI-based contextualization) that are likely to attract follow-up work, though immediate widespread adoption may be tempered by reliance on specific vector types/datasets and limited reporting of implementation details.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important problem—identifying unseen metaphors—by proposing a simple, general geometric contextualization that enables zero-shot classification using only individual word vectors (PMI vectors from Wikipedia). Demonstrating F-scores comparable to a fully supervised tensor model (Gutiérrez et al. 2016) and better than their semi‑supervised variant suggests this approach could change how researchers model contextual meaning for metaphors and encourage more work on per‑pair subspace methods. Its simplicity and reliance on widely available distributional vectors make it likely to be adopted and extended, though the lack of reported hyperparameters, code availability, and unstated limitations may slow immediate reproducibility and broader uptake.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles a consequential problem—identifying metaphors, including unseen adjective–noun pairs—and proposes a novel geometric, context-sensitive subspace method that enables zero-shot classification using only word vectors. Demonstrating F-scores comparable to a fully supervised tensor baseline (Gutiérrez et al. 2016) suggests this approach could meaningfully shift practice toward lightweight, generalizable models for metaphor detection. The method opens concrete new directions (pair-specific subspace induction, PMI-based contextualization) that are likely to attract follow-up work, though immediate widespread adoption may be tempered by reliance on specific vector types/datasets and limited reporting of implementation details.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against the key baselines from Gutiérrez et al. (2016) (supervised and semi‑supervised tensors) and simple baselines (majority and word2vec cosine) using the same adjective–noun dataset and F‑score metric, making the core comparisons fair. The authors explicitly acknowledge when their method is only comparable to the fully supervised baseline and superior to the semi‑supervised variant. However, the comparison set omits other relevant prior approaches addressing zero‑shot or novel metaphor extraction (e.g., works such as the 2013 extraction paper), and the paper provides limited methodological detail (hyperparameters/code not reported) that makes deeper, reproducible head‑to‑head analyses harder. Overall the comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper compares against the key supervised and semi-supervised tensor baselines from Gutiérrez et al. (2016) as well as simple baselines (majority and word2vec cosine), and uses the same adjective–noun dataset and F-score metric, so comparisons are largely fair. The authors explicitly report that their zero-shot geometric model attains F-scores comparable to the supervised tensor method and better than the semi-supervised variant, showing appropriate acknowledgment when prior work performs similarly. However, the comparison scope is somewhat narrow: there are earlier approaches targeting unseen or novel-metaphor extraction (e.g., work on robust extraction of novel metaphors) that were not evaluated against, and the paper does not report hyperparameter/code details that would aid reproducibility of the comparisons. Overall the empirical comparisons are solid but miss a few relevant competing methods and reproducibility details.

However, the comparison scope is somewhat narrow: there are earlier approaches targeting unseen or novel-metaphor extraction (e.g., work on robust extraction of novel metaphors) that were not evaluated against, and the paper does not report hyperparameter/code details that would aid reproducibility of the comparisons. Overall the empirical comparisons are solid but miss a few relevant competing methods and reproducibility details.

main_claims: Proposes a geometric, context-sensitive model that generates a new subspace for every candidate adjective-noun pair to identify metaphors.
The model requires only individual word vectors (no phrase representations), enabling zero-shot identification of unseen metaphors.

When analyzed via logistic regression on the produced subspaces, the method attains F-scores comparable to the fully supervised approach of Gutiérrez et al. (2016) and superior to their semi-supervised variant.
Model is simple and general, using PMI-based distributional vectors from Wikipedia and multiple k-dimensional subspace selection techniques.
_summary.methodology: Given word vectors (PMI co-occurrence vectors computed from the Dec 2014 English Wikipedia dump), the method selects k-dimensional subspaces for each adjective-noun pair (five selection techniques were tried) to project the pair into a contextually generated geometric space, and then applies logistic regression on features from that subspace to classify metaphor vs literal.
datasets: Gutiérrez et al. (2016) adjective-noun dataset (8,592 dyads, 23 adjectives)
December 2014 English Wikipedia dump (used to compute PMI word vectors)
baselines: Gutiérrez et al. (2016) supervised tensor method
Gutiérrez et al. (2016) semi-supervised tensor method
logistic regression on word2vec cosine similarity

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a PhD in computational linguistics; 8+ years building, evaluating, and deploying NLP models with emphasis on rigorous experimentation and reproducible workflows.
traits: carefully checks whether baselines are correctly implemented and comparable
looks for thorough ablation studies to isolate contributions of each component
values clear descriptions of data preprocessing, hyperparameters, and random seeds for reproducibility
assesses statistical significance, error analysis, and robustness to dataset or hyperparameter variation
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on experimental rigor, replicability, and whether the empirical claims are fully supported by the presented experiments and analyses.

reviewer_b.name: Applied Semantics Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor in NLP with long-standing work on figurative language (metaphor, irony) and distributional semantics; publishes on linguistic theory-informed computational models and their downstream impact.
traits: evaluates novelty relative to prior metaphor identification work and the conceptual fit with linguistic theories
weights practical applicability and how the approach generalizes to realistic language use beyond curated datasets
notices if the method advances understanding of metaphor representation or is mainly an engineering tweak
considers broader impact, including whether the approach could be integrated into downstream tasks or cognitive modeling
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Assesses the paper's novelty, theoretical contribution, and potential impact on the field and applied tasks, situating results within existing literature.
